title-group.article-title: Non-canonical autophagy in dermal dendritic cells mediates immunosuppressive effects of UV exposure

related-article.article-title: Retraction notice to Noncanonical autophagy in dermal dendritic cells mediates immunosuppressive effects of UV exposure J Allergy Clin Immunol Volume 145, Issue 5, May 2020, Pages 1389–1405
related-article.source: The Journal of allergy and clinical immunology

sec.title: BACKGROUND:
sec.p: Control of the inflammatory response is critical to maintaining homeostasis, and failure to do so contributes to the burden of chronic inflammation associated with several disease states. The mechanisms that underlie immunosuppression, however, remain largely unknown. While defects in autophagy machinery have been associated with inflammatory pathologies, we now appreciate that autophagic components participate in non-canonical pathways distinct from classical autophagy. We have previously demonstrated that LC3-associated phagocytosis (LAP), a non-canonical autophagic process dependent on Rubicon (RUBCN), contributes to immunosuppression.

sec.title: OBJECTIVE:
sec.p: We used Rubcn−/− mice to examine the role of in mediating the UV-induced immunotolerant program in a model of contact hypersensitivity (CHS).

sec.title: METHODS:
sec.p: Flow cytometry and transcriptional analysis was used to measure immune cell infiltration and activation in the skin of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice during the CHS response.

sec.title: RESULTS:
sec.p: Here, we demonstrate that LAP is required for UV-induced immunosuppression, and UV exposure induces a broadly anti-inflammatory transcriptional program dependent on Rubicon. Rubcn−/− mice are resistant to UV-induced immunosuppression and instead display exaggerated inflammation in a model of contact hypersensitivity (CHS). Specifically, RUBCN deficiency in CD301b+ dermal dendritic cells (dDC2s) results in their increased antigen presentation capacity and subsequent hyperactivation of the CD8+ T cell response.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS:
sec.p: LAP functions to limit the immune response and is critical in maintaining the balance between homeostasis and inflammation.

abstract.title: Graphical Abstract
abstract.p: Rubcn in dermal DC2s is required to mediate the immunosuppressive effects of UV exposure in a model of CHS.

abstract.title: CAPSULE SUMMARY
abstract.p: In this study, we demonstrate that RUBCN and LAP mediate the immunosuppressive effects of UV exposure in a model of skin inflammation by regulating antigen presentation and limiting activation of CD8+ T cells.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: To maintain homeostasis, the immune system must balance an appropriate activation of the immune response with the suppression of inappropriate inflammation. When confronted with stress, such as infection, injury or exposure to environmental triggers, the immune system must mount a response that can eliminate the threat, but ultimately return the system to its homeostatic state. Dysregulation of this homeostasis, even at a cellular level, can therefore have devastating inflammatory effects at the broader tissue and systemic level, resulting in chronic inflammation (1). As the prevalence of diseases associated with chronic inflammation is increasing, understanding the molecular mechanisms that mediate immunosuppression is needed to design more effective therapies.

The clearance of dying cells, a process termed efferocytosis, is the quintessential example of immunosuppression. The generation and subsequent controlled elimination of cells is a process that occurs constantly over the development and lifetime of an organism, and the immunosilent removal of these cells by phagocytes is key to the maintenance of homeostasis (2). Indeed, defects in the recognition, engulfment, or processing of dying cells have been associated with a variety of autoimmune and inflammatory disorders (3). LC3-associated phagocytosis (LAP) is a novel form of non-canonical autophagy recently described as a critical regulator of immunotolerant efferocytosis response. Deficiency of LAP, but not canonical autophagy, results in age-dependent development of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)-like inflammation in vivo associated with defective processing of dying cells (4–6). LAP, but not canonical autophagy, requires the activity of Rubicon (RUBCN), a protein that binds and regulates both the Class III PI3K complex and the NOX2 complex, both of which are essential for LAP (5). Thus, Rubcn−/− mice represent a valuable tool to allow the study of LAP-dependent mechanisms and pathologies without confounding co-defects in the canonical autophagy pathway (5).

To study how LAP and RUBCN mediate the immunosuppressive response to dying cells, we utilized a model of ultraviolet B light (UVB, hereafter referred to as UV) exposure of the skin, wherein the UV-mediated induction of apoptosis and the subsequent efferocytotic response mediates immunosuppression (7, 8). While UV exposure represents an environmental risk factor strongly associated with the development of skin cancer (9), UV as phototherapy is considered an effective treatment option for those with inflammatory skin disorders, such as psoriasis and allergic contact dermatitis (ACD), who fail to respond adequately to corticosteroids (10). How UV suppresses inflammation, though, remains, unclear. Studies have demonstrated UV exposure is thought to upregulate filaggrin, a filament associated protein critical for skin barrier integrity (11), and UV has also been shown to induce apoptosis of T cells in psoriatic lesions (12). Interestingly, up to 70% of SLE patients experience photosensitivity, wherein exposure to UV results in skin irritation and systemic inflammation (13). The term “lupus erythematosus” was originally coined to describe skin lesions, and approximately two-thirds of SLE patients suffer from skin inflammation, which is often a component of, if not a criterion for, broader systemic autoimmune disorders (14). As UV exposure is a classical inducer of immunosuppression in healthy individuals, yet a common trigger for inflammation in SLE patients, we utilized the Rubcn−/− mice, who are susceptible to SLE-like disease, to explore the role of the LAP pathway in mediating immunosuppression upon UV treatment. Further, we examined the pathological aberrations in UV-mediated immunosuppression using the contact hypersensitivity (CHS) model, a well-characterized mouse model of ACD (8). Collectively, we demonstrate that RUBCN is required to establish the immunotolerant response during UV exposure, and failure to do so results in exacerbated and prolonged inflammation.

sec.title: Mice
sec.p: Mice were housed and bred in a specific-pathogen-free animal facility at NIEHS/NIH. The study was approved by the NIEHS Animal Care and Use Committee (ACUC). Mice used in the experiments were 8–12 weeks old females. Age and weight-matched littermates were used as wild type controls. All mouse strains were maintained on a C57BL/6 background. The mice were sustained under regulated conditions with diamond bedding, food, and water ad libitum.

Rubcn−/− mice have been previously described (5). Ulk1−/− mice were provided by M. Kundu (St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital, Memphis, TN, USA) (15). Atg7flox/flox mice were provided by M. Komatsu (Tokyo Metropolitan Institute of Medical Science, Tokyo, Japan) (16) and bred to Cd11c-Cre+ and LysM-Cre+ mice (described below) to generate CD11c-Cre+ Atg7flox/flox.

SJL-Ptprca Pepcb/BoyJ (CD45.1+ B6, Jax Stock No. 002014) and C57BL/6J (Jax Stock No. 000664) mice were purchased from Jackson Laboratories (Bar Harbor, ME, USA) and were used as donor mice for adoptive transfer experiments described below.

sec.title: Generation of Rubcnflox/flox transgenic mouse strains
sec.p: Conditional null (“flox”) Rubicon mice were generated from embryonic stem cells purchased from the European Conditional Mouse Mutagenesis Program (EUCOMM Project 76467). The endogenous Rubcn locus is located on chromosome 16 and has two dominant transcript variants that differ by the in-phase splicing of an additional 45 base-pair exon after exon 7. To generate a mouse line with Cre-inducible genetic disruption of Rubcn, LoxP sites were introduced flanking exon 6 (See Figure S9A). Excision of exon 6 will result in an N-terminus truncated protein similar to the previously published null allele that has a translation stop at amino acid D188.

Briefly, a targeting vector was generated with a 5.9 kb 5’ homology arm; an FRT-flanked EUCOMM “knockout first” cassette (splice acceptor-LacZ/βgal-polyA; PGK-NeoR); LoxP-site flanked exon 6; a 3.3 kb 3’ homology arm; and a PGK-DTA negative selection cassette outside the arms of homology. C57BL/6 Agouti (JM8A3.N1) ES cells were electroporated with the linearized targeting vector, and G418-resistant ES clones were screened by long-range PCR. Sequence integrity of the recombinase sites and exon 6 in targeted ES clones was confirmed by amplicon sequencing (screening/genotyping primers Table X). ES clone B03 was microinjected into C57BL/6J wildtype blastocyst and non-surgically transferred to SWISS surrogate females. Chimeric F0 founders were bred to albino B6J females, and agouti F1 offspring were screened by short-range PCR sequencing. The FRT-flanked LacZ-NeoR cassette was excised by breeding to germline FLP expressing mice purchased from Jackson Labs (B6.129S4-Gt(ROSA)26Sor<tm1(FLP1)Dym>, JAX Stock 009086, PMID: 11105051). The resulting Rubcn conditional null (“flox”) allele was maintained by perpetual backcross to wildtype C57BL/6N. To confirm the null status of the Cre-recombined allele, Rubcn flox mice were bred to germline expressing Cre mice purchased from JAX (B6.C-Tg(CMV-cre)1Cgn, Jax Stock: 006054, PMID: 8559668), and subsequently in-crossed to generated Rubcn exon 6-deficient mice. In cDNA from exon 6-deficient mice, RT-PCR readily identified exon 5 to exon 7 splicing, which results in an immediate frameshift/missense mutation and a translation stop within exon 7. The next in-phase exon after exon 5 is exon 10, which would result in a RUBCN protein variant with a 281 amino acid internal deletion. However, aside from expected exon 5 to exon 7 splicing, no other splice variants were identified in exon 6-deficient mice.

Rubcn floxed mice were bred to Cd11c-Cre+ mice purchased from JAX (B6.Cg-Tg(Itgaxcre)1–1Reiz/J, Jax Stock: 008068, PMID: 17591855) and LysM-Cre+ mice purchased from JAX B6.129P2-Lyz2tm1(cre)Ifo/J, Jax Stock: 004781, PMID: 10621974).

sec.title: Contact hypersensitivity model
sec.p: Contact hypersensitivity (CHS) model was utilized as previously described (17). See Schematic below. Briefly, the back skin of anesthetized mice was shaved, followed by topical application of 0.5% 2,4-Dinitro-1-fluorobenzene (DNFB, 50 μl in 4:1 acetone/olive oil) to the dorsal skin (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) (sensitization). After 4 days, mice were challenged with topical application of 0.2–0.3% DNFB or vehicle only (5 μl each on the dorsal and ventral side of the ear pinnae). To evaluate the effect of UV pre-exposure, shaved back skin of mice were exposed to 67.5 mJ/cm2 of UVB (290 – 320 nm) per dose using a UV lamp (Spectroline, Westbury, NY, USA) for 3 consecutive days prior to sensitization, described above . During UV dose-response experiments, shaved back skin of mice were exposed to 33.75 mJ/cm2 or 135mJ/cm2 of UVB per dose, described above. Ears were shielded from UV exposure. Ear thickness was measured daily using an engineer’s electronic digital micrometer (Marathon, San Juan Capistrano, CA, USA) in microns, and ear swelling was calculated as the percentage increase in ear thickness over day 0 measurement. Tissue was harvested at various timepoints for flow cytometry, RNA isolation, and histology. No less than three independent experiments were performed, with 5–7 replicates per condition per timepoint.

In some cases, the back skin of anesthetized mice was shaved, followed by sensitization with 0.5% DNFB to the dorsal skin on day 0. To evaluate the effect of UV exposure post-sensitization, shaved back skin of mice was exposed to 67.5 mJ/cm2 of UVB (290 – 320 nm) per dose using a UV lamp on days 1–3 post-sensitization. On day 4, mice were challenged with topical application of 0.2–0.3% DNFB or vehicle only on the dorsal and ventral side of the ear pinnae, described above. See Figure S7A.

sec.title: Adoptive transfer experiments
sec.p: Single cell suspensions of CD8+ or CD4+ T cells from lymph nodes (LNs) and spleens of the naïve donor mice were isolated using the EasySep™ mouse CD4+ or CD8+ T cell Isolation Kit, respectively (STEMCELL Technologies Inc., Vancouver, Canada). 10 × 106 donor T cells were injected retro-orbitally into the recipient Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice. See Figures 4A and S6A. In some cases, isolated mouse CD8+ T cells were stained with the Tag-it Violet™ dye (Cat # 425101, Biolegend, San Diego, CA, USA), per manufacturer’s protocol to track the proliferation of CD8+ T cells in vivo.

sec.title: Nanostring analysis
sec.p: Nanostring Mouse nCounter® Immunology Code Set panel (Nanostring Technologies, Seattle, WA, USA) was performed on 50 μg RNA extracted from skin described above. Hybridization and nCounter were performed according to the manufacturer’s protocol. In brief, reactions were hybridized for 20 hours at 65 °C, after which the products were used to run on the nCounter preparation station for removal of excess probes. Data were collected with the nCounter digital analyzer by counting individual barcodes. Data generated from the nCounter digital analyzer were examined with the nCounter digital analyzer software system v2.1.1. Data were normalized to the geometric means of spiked-in positive controls (controls for assay efficiency) and spiked-in negative controls (normalized for background). The data were further normalized to the housekeeping genes- Gapdh, Hprt, and Tubb5. Nanostring RNA counts were analyzed with the Partek Genomic Suite, to identify significantly regulated probes (p≤0.05 via two-way ANOVA with multiple comparisons test [MCT]). Ingenuity Pathway Analysis software (Qiagen, Venlo, The Netherlands) was used for whole transcriptome microarray data analysis was performed. Data is represented as average fold change. Heatmaps of Nanostring data were generated with the R studio or GraphPad Prism.

sec.title: Microarray Analysis
sec.p: Gene expression analysis was conducted using Agilent Whole Mouse Genome 4×44 Version 2 multiplex format oligo arrays (026655) (Agilent Technologies Santa Clara, CA, USA). Eleven nanograms (11 ng) of total RNA were amplified as directed in the NuGEN Ovation Pico WTA System and 5 μg of amplified cDNA was enzymatically labeled using the Agilent SureTag DNA Labeling Kit. For each sample, 3 μg of Cy3 labeled cDNA was hybridized for 17 hours in a rotating hybridization oven. Slides were washed and then scanned with an Agilent Scanner. Data were obtained using the Agilent Feature Extraction software (v12), using the 1-color defaults for all parameters. The Agilent Feature Extraction Software performed error modeling, adjusting for additive and multiplicative noise. The resulting data were processed using OmicSoft Array Studio (Version 10) software. Genes with fold change of +/− 1.5 and significance of p<0.05 (two-way ANOVA with MCT) of Rubcn−/− versus Rubcn+/+ dDC2s were considered significant, and analyzed using Ingenuity Pathway Analysis and DAVID analysis tools. Heatmaps were generated with R studio and GraphPad Prism.

sec.title: Histology, Immunohistochemistry, and Immunofluorescence
sec.p: Tissues were collected, placed in the histology cassettes, immediately fixed in 10% formalin for 48 hours at 4°C, and then transferred to 70% ethanol containing chamber. Enzyme-induced epitope retrieval was performed using a Proteinase K (DakoCytomation, Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) for 90 seconds at RT, after which endogenous peroxidase blocking was done by immersing the sections in 3% H2O2 for 15 minutes. The tissues were embedded in paraffin blocks for sectioning and were stained for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) for histopathologic evaluation or anti-phospho-H2A.X (Ser139) (Cat # 4309, Cell Signaling, Danvers, MA, USA), Sections were stained with Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labeling (TUNEL), anti-cleaved caspase-3 antibody (the active form of caspase-3), Masson’s trichrome (Poly Scientific R&D Corp, Bay Shore, NY, USA) (18), or anti-connective tissue growth factor (CTGF) antibody (Atlas Antibodies, Bromma, Sweden) for immunohistochemistry. Tissue fibrosis, as well as the other microscopic lesions, were graded by a board-certified veterinary pathologist on a scale of normal (0), minimal (+1), mild (+2), moderate (+3), or marked (+4). For TUNEL staining, ApopTag Plus Peroxidase In Situ Apoptosis Detection Kit (MilliporeSigma, Burlington, MA, USA) was used to stain the tissue using the manufacturer’s guidelines. The TdT enzyme incubation was performed for 15 minutes at 37°C at a dilution of 1:5 (11 μl dH2O, 77 μl Reaction buffer, 22 μl TdT per 110 μl.) Staining was visualized using 3-diaminobenzidine (DAB) chromogen (DakoCytomation, Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and counterstained with hematoxylin. The slides were dehydrated by using graded ethanol, cleared in xylene, and then coverslips were mounted.

For CD8+ T cells and CD301b+ dDCs staining, tissue slides were fixed in 3% paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 10 minutes, washed 3 times with PBS for 5 minutes, and then permeabilized in 0.1% Triton X-100 in PBS. Slides were blocked using 2% BSA in PBS for 30 minutes, Slides were incubated with 1:100 dilution of mouse anti-CD301b PE/dazzle (Cat # 146815, Biolegend, San Diego, CA, USA) or mouse anti-CD8 (Cat # sc-1177, Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Dallas, TX, USA), followed by anti-mouse AlexaFluor-647 (Cat# A27040, Invitrogen, Thermo-Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The slides were washed 3 times in PBS for 5 minutes and the coverslips were mounted in ProLong™ Gold Antifade Mountant with DAPI (Life Technologies, Thermo-Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA).

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: For ear swelling, multiple Student’s T-tests for independent samples were performed for statistical evaluations and to obtain the statistical significance values using GraphPad Prism 7 software (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA, USA). For flow cytometry, two-way ANOVA with Sidak multiple comparison tests (MCT) were performed for statistical evaluations and to obtain the statistical significance values using GraphPad Prism 7 software (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA, USA). Statistical analysis for Nanostring and microarray array datasets were performed using Partek genomics suite software (Partek Incorporated, St. Louis, MO, USA).
Additional Methods are available in the Online Repository.

sec.title: Rubcn is required to effectively initiate the UV-mediated immunotolerant program
sec.p: UV exposure triggers a robust immunosuppressive transcriptional program (19), and both Rubcn mRNA and protein expression in UV-exposed back skin of wild type mice was significantly upregulated (Figure 1A), consistent with its role in immunosuppression (4, 20). To determine the pathways disrupted in Rubcn−/− skin in response to UV treatment, we used Nanostring transcriptional analysis of 547 genes involved in inflammation and immunity (21) to compare the expression of immune genes at baseline and post-UV treatment. Basally, Rubcn−/− skin displayed reduced expression of key inflammatory genes, compared to untreated Rubcn+/+ skin (Figure S1A). However, in the absence of Rubcn, UV exposure induced a highly inflammatory program, consisting of increased expression of genes involved in inflammation (Rela, Nfkb2, Il18), T cell activation (Icos, Cd4, Grzmb), and DC maturation and antigen presentation (Icosl, Cd40, Ciita) (Figure 1B), compared to UV-treated Rubcn+/+ skin. The expression of these genes in UV-exposed Rubcn−/− skin was significantly increased compared to basal Rubcn+/+ skin, suggesting an overall increase in the inflammatory state. Thus, UV exposure induces a dramatic transcriptional upregulation of inflammatory programs in Rubcn−/− skin (Figure S1B). This exacerbated inflammatory gene profile was not due to increased tissue damage (H&E) (Figure 1C) or cell death (TUNEL [Figure 1D] or cleaved caspase 3 [Figure 1E] immunohistochemical staining), induced by UV exposure in Rubcn−/− skin, and both Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− skin displayed comparable amounts of epidermal hyperplasia, hyperkeratosis, dermal fibrosis, and inflammation in response to UV (Figure 1F). To further confirm that Rubcn deficiency does not impart increased susceptibility to UV-induced damage, we subjected Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− murine embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs), a primary cellular source capable of differentiation into skin (22), to UV exposure. Both genotypes displayed comparable levels of phospho-H2γAX, a marker of double-stranded DNA breaks (23, 24) (Figure S1C) and comparable rates and levels of cell death, as evidenced by SYTOX Green fluorescence post-UV exposure (Figure S1D). Collectively, UV exposure initiated vastly different immunological programs in Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− skin, indicating that Rubicon is important for establishing the immunosuppressive state in response to UV exposure.

sec.title: UV pre-exposure fails to induce immunosuppression in Rubcn−/− mice
sec.p: The controlled exposure of skin to UV, a process termed phototherapy, can be an effective treatment for inflammatory skin disorders (25, 26), such as ACD, the most prevalent type of dermatological immunotoxicity in humans (27, 28). CHS is a well-characterized, biphasic mouse model of allergic contact dermatitis, and inflammation associated with CHS can be suppressed with UV pre-exposure (8). During sensitization, allergens (or haptens) are topically applied to the skin, where they gain immunogenicity after internalization by skin-resident antigen presenting cells (APCs), notably dermal DCs (dDC1s and dDC2s) and Langerhans cells (LCs) (17, 29–31). Hapten-activated APCs traffic to skin-draining lymph node (dLNs) where they activate CD4+ and CD8+ T cells. During elicitation, re-exposure to the same hapten at a neutral skin site triggers a robust activation of this primed T cell response, resulting in localized inflammation, irritation, and edema (28).

As UV exposure of Rubcn−/− skin resulted in increased inflammation, we examined if this hyperinflammatory state in Rubcn−/− mice would translate into increased inflammation, as opposed to protection, in the CHS model. The back skin (sensitization site) of mice was exposed to UV for three consecutive days, during which time the ears (elicitation site) were protected from UV exposure. In the sensitization phase, DNFB was topically applied to either control or UV-treated back skin of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− female mice. Four days later, during elicitation, DNFB was applied topically to the ear, a distal skin site that had not been previously exposed to DNFB or UV, and ear swelling was measured (17). Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice displayed comparable ear thickness changes (i.e., swelling) and resolution in response to DNFB only treatment (Figure 2A), while untreated contralateral ears displayed no swelling in response to DNFB treatment (Figure S2A). As expected, exposure of the back skin of Rubcn+/+ mice to UV prior to sensitization resulted in significant suppression of DNFB-induced ear inflammation during elicitation (Figure 2A–B). However, UV pre-exposure not only failed to suppress inflammation in Rubcn−/− mice but rather exacerbated DNFB-associated ear swelling (Figure 2A–B). UV-mediated immunosuppression was evident histologically in ears from Rubcn+/+ mice by decreased severity of inflammation when compared to DNFB application alone (Figure 2C–D), with UV pre-treatment significantly reducing severities of inflammation, necrosis, and hyperplasia (Figure 2D). Ears from UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice, on the other hand, displayed more severe inflammation, hyperplasia, hyperkeratosis, necrosis, and fibrosis when compared to DNFB application alone or UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice (Figure 2C–D, S2B). Despite being protected from UV exposure, ear tissue from UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice also contained more TUNEL+ cells compared to Rubcn+/+ ears (Figure 2C), consistent with the ability of uncontrolled inflammation to induce cell death (32) and RUBCN’s essential role in efferocytosis (4). This increased inflammation was dependent on dose of UV, as Rubcn−/− mice that received a milder UV dose (33.75 mJ/cm2) did not exhibit increased inflammation, but Rubcn−/− mice receiving the standard 67.5 mJ/cm2 or increased 135 mJ/cm2 UV dose exhibited significantly increased ear swelling compared to DNFB only-treated ears (Figure S2C). Increased inflammation and/or edema persisted for approximately two weeks post-elicitation, as ear thickness from UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice failed to return to baseline levels, whereas the increased ear thickness of UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice resolved after approximately two weeks (Figure S2D). Taken together, RUBCN plays a role in mediating the immunosuppressive effects of UV exposure, and failure to engage RUBCN’s activity can result in exacerbated inflammation.

While canonical autophagy and LAP share many molecular components, LAP proceeds independently of the autophagy pre-initiation complex, composed of ATG13, FIP200, and ULK1 (6). In addition, RUBCN has been described as a negative regulator of canonical autophagy (5, 33, 34), and Rubcn−/− macrophages exhibit increased autophagosome formation in response to autophagic stimuli, such as rapamycin (5). To confirm that UV-mediated immunosuppression was indeed a LAP-dependent event, the ability of UV pre-exposure to suppress the DNFB-induced CHS reaction in Ulk1−/− mice was assessed. UV pre-treatment was able to suppress swelling equivalently in the elicited ears of Ulk1+/+ and Ulk1−/− mice (Figure 2E–F), while vehicle only contralateral ears remained uninflamed (Figure S4E), indicating that LAP, not canonical autophagy, is required for UV-mediated immunosuppression.

sec.title: UV pre-exposure fails to suppress immune cell infiltration in Rubcn−/− mice
sec.p: To further examine the inflammatory environment shaped by Rubcn deficiency, we performed Nanostring transcriptional analysis of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− ear skin after elicitation with or without UV pre-exposure. While elicited ear skin from UV-pretreated Rubcn+/+ mice upregulated genes consistent with immunosuppression, including Ikbkb, Ikbkg, and Abcb10, while Rubcn−/− ear skin upregulated a pro-inflammatory genetic program, including Hif1a, Cd8a, H2-K1, and Cd86, similar to the transcriptional profile observed in Rubcn−/− UV-treated back skin (Figure S3A).

We next investigated if this heightened inflammatory transcriptional profile in UV-exposed Rubcn−/− mice correlated with an increase in immune cell infiltration and activation. After elicitation, DNFB-treated ears from both Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice contained increased numbers of innate immune cells, including macrophages (MΘs), neutrophils (NΘs), and mast cells (Figure 3A, gating strategy is shown in S3B-C and representative flow cytometry plots shown in S4A-C). UV pre-exposure significantly reduced the MΘ, NΘ, and mast cell populations in DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ ears, consistent with reduced inflammation in UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ ear skin (Figure 3A). However, UV pre-treatment did not inhibit recruitment of MΘs and NΘs to DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− ears, though mast cells were efficiently suppressed by UV exposure in Rubcn−/− mice (Figure 3A). In addition to ubiquitous immune cells, the skin contains specialized resident immune cells, such as CD207+ CD11c+ CD11b+ Langerhans cells (LCs), CD103+ CD207+ CD11c+ CD11blo dermal DC1s (dDC1s), and CD301b+ CD207lo CD11c+ CD11b+ dermal DC2s (dDC2s) (35), and all three subsets of cells have been implicated in CHS pathology (36). In response to DNFB treatment alone, both Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− ears contained equivalent levels of LCs, dDC1s, and dDC2s. Ears from UV pre-treated Rubcn+/+ mice contained equivalent or slightly fewer LCs and dDC1s, and significantly less dDC2s, compared to DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ ears (Figure 3B, gating strategy is shown in S3B-C and representative flow cytometry plots shown in S4D-E). UV pre-exposure did not increase the levels of LCs or dDC1s in Rubcn−/− ears, though the percentage of LCs in UV+DNFB treated Rubcn−/− ears was higher than that of in UV+DNFB treated Rubcn+/+ ears (Figure 3B). Strikingly, while UV pre-exposure was able to suppress the dDC2 population in Rubcn+/+ mice, ears from UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice contained significantly increased levels of dDC2s, compared to both DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice and UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice (Figure 3B–C).

Flow cytometric analysis of RUBCN protein expression also revealed that dDC2s express significantly more RUBCN than any other cellular subset examined (Figure S4F). Recent studies have demonstrated that dDC2s are an important antigen presenting cells in the CHS reaction (36–38), and as UV pre-treatment of Rubcn−/− mice failed to suppress dDC2s at sites of elicitation, we theorized that increased dDC2 presence would result in increased infiltration and activation of T cells during elicitation. The primary effectors of inflammation in the CHS reaction are CD8+ and CD4+ T cells, and classically, increased dDC2 activity is associated with increased CD8+ T cell responses (39). UV pre-treatment significantly suppressed the recruitment and activation, as measured by the production of IFNγ, of CD8+ T cells in Rubcn+/+ ears after elicitation, whereas this suppression was absent in UV pre-treated Rubcn−/− mice (Figure 3D–E, gating strategy and representative flow cytometry plots shown in S5A-B). Rather, CD8+ T cells were more abundant and produced more displayed increased effector function (IFNγ) in UV pre-treated Rubcn−/− ears after elicitation, compared to DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− ears and UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ ears (Figure 3D–E).

Th1 and Th17 CD4+ T cells have also been implicated in CHS pathology (40). UV pre-exposure of Rubcn−/− mice failed to suppress recruitment of Th1 and Th17 cells after elicitation, though UV did not result in an increase in percentages of Th1 and Th17 CD4+ T cells, compared to DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− mice (Figure 3F, gating strategy and representative flow cytometry plots shown in S5A,C-D). The generation of regulatory T cells (Tregs) mitigates some of the inflammation associated with CHS (41, 42), and as such, UV exposure increased the percentage of Treg cells in Rubcn+/+ ears post-elicitation, consistent with their role in suppressing CHS-mediated inflammation (43, 44). However, UV pre-treatment did not induce a more robust Treg response in Rubcn−/− ear skin, rather the percentage of Treg cells in UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− skin was decreased compared to DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− ear skin (Figure 3F). No effect was observed for Th2 cells, as previously described (45) (Figure S5A, F). Thus, in the absence of LAP, UV pre-exposure results in the increased recruitment and activation of effector cells, such as CD8+ T cells, Th1, and Th17 cells, and a decreased Treg response, with a net effect of hyperinflammation after elicitation during the CHS model.

sec.title: Rubcn imparts immunosuppression in a T cell-extrinsic manner
sec.p: While Rubcn is ubiquitously expressed (5), its expression level varies across cell types, with T cells having the lowest level of expression among the cells examined (Figure S4F). To exclude a T cell-intrinsic role for Rubicon, CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells from naïve wild type C57BL/6 CD45.1+ mice were transferred into Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− recipients after UV exposure but before DNFB sensitization and elicitation (Figure 4A). Ears from UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice displayed increased ear swelling and contained a significantly higher percentage of CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells, compared to affected ears of UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice (Figure 4B). Moreover, CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells transferred into UV pre-treated Rubcn−/− hosts adopted a more effector phenotype after elicitation, as demonstrated by increased IFNγ and CD44, compared to CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells from a UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ host (Figure 4B). Transfer of naïve CD45.1+ CD4+ T cells into Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− recipients (Figure S6A), however, failed to significantly suppress ear inflammation in either Rubcn,+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice (Figure S6B), suggesting that the dominant pathway in which LAP mediates UV-induced immunosuppression is through the inhibition of the CD8+ T cell response.

We next analyzed the ability of naïve CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells to become activated post-sensitization in the dLNs of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice. UV pre-exposure reduced the percentage and activation of CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells in the dLNs of Rubcn+/+ mice after sensitization, compared to DNFB only-treated Rubcn+/+ mice. However, dLNs from UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice contained a significantly higher percentage of CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells post-sensitization, compared to both DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− mice and UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice (Figure S6C). Moreover, these CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells had adopted a more effector phenotype and were significantly more activated, as evidenced by increased IFNγ production (Figure S6C-D).

We investigated if a UV-exposed Rubcn−/− host could elicit a more robust elicitation response from antigen-experienced, wild type CD8+ T cells. We utilized an adoptive transfer model, wherein C57BL/6 mice were sensitized with DNFB, and CD8+ T cells were harvested from the dLNs, stained with proliferation dye, and transferred into untreated or UV pre-treated recipients. The CHS response was elicited in recipients with DNFB or vehicle application on contralateral ears, and UV-mediated suppression of ear swelling was assessed (Figure 4C). UV pre-treated Rubcn−/− mice displayed significantly increased antigen-specific inflammation in ear tissue after elicitation, compared to both UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice and DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− mice (Figure 4D). UV pre-treatment of Rubcn+/+ mice suppressed the recruitment of wild type, DNFB-experienced CD8+ T cells to the affected ear, whereas CD8+ T cells in UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice displayed significantly increased recruitment to the treated ear (Figure 4E). Furthermore, UV pre-treatment of Rubcn−/− mice failed to suppress the activation of wild type DNFB-educated CD8+ T cells after elicitation, resulting in significantly higher expression of CD44 and production of IFNγ (Figure 4E). Similarly, UV pre-treatment of Rubcn−/− mice also failed to inhibit proliferation of wild type DNFB-experienced CD8+ T cells, compared to UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ mice (Figure 4F). These results indicate that the inflammatory environment triggered by UV exposure of Rubcn−/− mice is capable of eliciting a more robust antigen-specific response from T cells generated in a wild type environment. Therefore, while T cell activation during sensitization or elicitation does not require Rubcn-intrinsic activity, UV pre-exposure of Rubcn−/− mice triggers the hyperactivation of CD8+ T cells, presumably via RUBCN activity in antigen presenting cells.

sec.title: Rubcn is required in CD301b+ dDC2s to limit activation and antigen presentation during sensitization.
sec.p: Having established that RUBCN plays a T cell-extrinsic role during the CHS reaction, we therefore hypothesized that RUBCN plays a role in antigen presenting cells. Further, our Nanostring transcriptional analysis indicates that antigen presentation and inflammatory pathways in innate immune cells were enhanced in the absence of Rubcn in response to UV exposure. As we hypothesized that UV exposure prior to sensitization was required for immunosuppression, we treated Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice with UV after sensitization, but before elicitation (Figure S7A). This UV exposure regimen did not result in decreased ear swelling in either Rubcn+/+ or Rubcn−/− mice (Figure S7B), indicating that UV exposure is required prior to sensitization to mediate its Rubcn-dependent immunosuppressive effects.

While dDC1s, dDC2s, and LCs can take up hapten and activate effector T cell populations, such as CD8+ T cells, Th1, and Th17 cells, the most salient studies demonstrate that dDC2s are the most critical antigen presenting cells during the CHS reaction (36–38). Hence, we next examined the Rubcn−/− antigen presenting cells during the sensitization phase of the CHS model. In Rubcn+/+ mice, migration of LCs to dLNs post-sensitization was significantly inhibited by UV pre-treatment; however, UV pre-exposure failed to inhibit LC trafficking to the dLNs of Rubcn−/− mice after sensitization (Figure 5A, representative flow cytometry plots shown in S8A). Similarly, CD86 and MHCI expression on Rubcn+/+ LCs in the dLNs was suppressed by UV exposure, but these activation markers were not decreased in Rubcn−/− LCs. However, UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− LCs recruitment and activation were not increased compared to DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− LCs (Figure 5A). Similar to their activity in the skin, UV exposure suppressed Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− dDC1s in terms of recruitment and activation (Figure 5B, representative flow cytometry plots shown in S8B).

After sensitization, dDC2s from both Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− back skin traffic equivalently to dLNs and express comparable levels of the activation markers, CD86, MHC-I, and MHC-II (Figure 5C, representative flow cytometry plots shown in S8C). While UV pre-treatment significantly inhibited recruitment Rubcn+/+ dDC2s to the dLNs post-sensitization, trafficking of Rubcn−/− dDC2s to the dLNs significantly increased after UV+DNFB treatment (Figure 5C). In addition, UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− dDC2s in the dLNs had higher expression of CD86, MHC-I, and MHC-II, compared to both UV+DNFB Rubcn+/+ dDC2s and DNFB only-treated Rubcn−/− dDC2s (Figure 5C). This increased activation of UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− dDC2s also correlated with increased CD8+ and CD4+ T cell percentage and activation in the dLNs of UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice after sensitization (Figure S8D).

The activation and function of dDC2s are positively associated with the activation of CD8+ T cells, the primary effector in CHS (46). As dDC2s were significantly increased and more activated in UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn−/− mice post-sensitization and also had the highest expression of RUBCN among the cell types we examined (Figure S4F), we hypothesized that Rubcn mediates the anti-inflammatory effect of UV exposure by limiting the trafficking and activation of dDC2s. To assess this, we isolated dDC2s and LCs from the dLNs of UV+DNFB-treated Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice post-sensitization and performed microarray analysis to explore the Rubcn-dependent transcriptional response to UV during sensitization (Figure S8E). In response to UV+DNFB treatment, Rubcn−/− dDC2s significantly upregulated genes associated with antigen presentation and DC maturation (Cd40, H2-K2, B2m) (Figure 5D) and inflammation (Ccr7, Ifnar1, Il12b, Il1b), compared to Rubcn+/+ dDC2s (Figure 5E). However, both Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− LCs, express equivalent levels of these key transcripts (Figure 5D–E). Taken together, these data indicate the Rubcn in dDC2s is required to mitigate their activation and antigen presentation activity in a model of skin inflammation, and the absence of Rubcn in dDC2s results in increased inflammation.

sec.title: Rubcn expression in CD11c+ cells is required for UV suppression of inflammation
sec.p: Finally, we investigated if disruption of LAP within CD11c+ dendritic cells alone could account for the phenotype we observe in the constitutive Rubcn knockout. To study this, we used a conditional knockout approach for the disruption of Rubcn gene expression via CD11c promoter-expressed Cre recombinase (Figure S9A). While Cd11c-Cre strains have been used to study deletion in DCs, primarily, some studies have used Cd11c-Cre strains to examine deletion in LCs (47). As the locus of the floxed gene can influence deletion efficiency, we first used flow cytometry to examine the deletion efficiency of Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice. As expected, B cells, T cells, mast cells, neutrophils, macrophages, and LCs expressed equivalent levels of RUBCN in both Rubcnflox/flox mice and Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice. However, dDC1s and dDC2s from Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice. displayed a significant RUBCN deletion, compared to those from Rubcnflox/flox mice (Figure S9B), consistent with previous reports (48). UV pre-treatment suppressed ear swelling in wild type, Cre-negative (Rubcnflox/flox) mice, whereas Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice displayed significantly increased ear swelling in response to UV+DNFB treatment (Figure 6A–B). Conversely, UV pre-treatment effectively suppressed ear swelling in both Rubcnflox/flox and LysM-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice, in which Rubcn is deleted in myeloid cells (4) (Figure S10A-B). Similar trends were also observed in the Cd11c-Cre+ Atg7flox/flox and LysM-Cre+ Atg7flox/flox mice, where the downstream autophagy- and LAP-dependent gene, Atg7 (6, 49), is deleted in dDC2s and myeloid cells, respectively (Figure S10C-D).

After elicitation, UV pre-exposure successfully limited dDC2 numbers and activation in Rubcnflox/flox mice, while dDC2s were more abundant and activated in UV+DNFB-treated Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice, compared to both UV+DNFB-treated Rubcnflox/flox mice and DNFB only-treated Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice (Figure 6C). Similar to the constitutive Rubcn knockout, dDC1s were sufficiently suppressed in both Rubcnflox/flox and Cd11c- Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice. Importantly, UV pre-treatment was able to suppress LC levels and activity in the inflamed ears of both Rubcnflox/flox and Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice (Figure 6C), supporting data that Cd11c-Cre-mediated deletion is absent in LCs (Figure S9B). Functional suppression of LCs via UV exposure was not sufficient to rescue the inflammatory phenotype observed in the Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice, indicating that Rubcn in dDC2s is critical for establishing the immunotolerant state.

After elicitation, ears from UV+DNFB-treated Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice also contained an increased percentage of IFNγ-producing CD8+ T cells, consistent with our previous data (Figure 6D). UV pre-treatment also failed to suppress Th1 and Th17 CD4+ T cells in the ears of Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice, though these mice did exhibit an increase in the percentage of Treg cells in inflamed ear tissue (Figure 6E), consistent with the activity of functional LCs (50). Thus, the overall T cell composition within the affected tissue of UV+DNFB-treated Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice was skewed to inflammatory, effector cells, specifically CD8+ T cells. Linear regression analysis revealed that increases in dDC2s were positively correlated to increases in CD8+ T cells in both UV+DNFB-treated Rubcnflox/flox and Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice (Figure S10E). Thus, despite significant differences in the magnitude of responses between genotypes, dDC2 presence was associated increased CD8+ T cell numbers.

Interestingly, re-analysis of microarray data of human skin cellular subsets revealed increased expression of RUBCN in dDCs and LCs (51–53). Expression of genes common to both LAP and canonical autophagy, such as BECN1, PIK3C3 (VPS34), ATG5, ATG12, ATG16L1, and ATG7 (Figure S11A), and genes required for canonical autophagy only, such as ULK1 and RB1CC1 (FIP200) (Figure S11B), were equivalent among the human skin cellular subsets CD141+ dDCs, CD1c+ dDCs, CD14+ dDCs, LCs, and MΘs. However, expression of RUBCN, required only for LAP, was increased in CD141+ dDCs, CD1c+ dDCs, and LCs (Figure S11C). Both CD141+ DCs and CD1c+ DCs subsets arise from a common precursor, and both have shown to play a role in activating T cells, with CD141+ dDCs most closely resembling dDC1s and CD1c+ dDCs most closely resembling dDC2s (51, 54–56). Collectively, this data suggests that LAP and RUBCN may play an important and distinct role in these human skin cell subsets, similar to its role in murine dDCs.

Taken together, these data demonstrate that the non-canonical autophagy pathway, LAP, is required to establish the anti-inflammatory state induced by UV exposure, and defects in the ability to mount immunosuppression results in hyperactivation of dDC2s, and subsequently increased effector function of T cells. Thus, RUBCN represents a key mediator of immunosuppression during models of inflammation.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: While inflammation is an essential physiological process, how an organism recalibrates after stress to return to homeostasis is equally critical, as chronic inflammation underlies a multitude of pathological conditions (1). We now appreciate that the balance between homeostasis and inflammation is not necessarily a linear function, but a spectrum of acceptable ranges that evolve with exposure. Indeed, the nature and intensity of stress can shift the goalposts for homeostasis, forcing organisms to adapt to their new normal (57). Defects in the autophagic machinery have long been linked to aberrant inflammatory responses (58–63). Recent work demonstrates that a form of non-canonical autophagy, LC3-associated phagocytosis (LAP), is a critical regulator of immunotolerance and a process functionally and molecularly distinct from traditional autophagy (4–6, 64, 65). Identification of the LAP-specific gene, Rubcn, and generation of the Rubcn−/− mouse as a model of LAP-deficiency (but intact autophagy) has allowed us to explore the significance of LAP in physiological and pathological settings (5). Here, we demonstrate that the environmental stimuli, UV exposure, requires non-canonical autophagy to establish the immunosuppressive state. In the absence of Rubcn, UV exposure induces a robust inflammatory state, which results in a hyperactivated immune response and delayed resolution. Thus, LAP is integral in mediating the balance between inflammation and homeostasis.

The skin represents both the frontline of immune defense and the most exposed organ, tasked with maintaining homeostasis in the face of a constant stream of environmental insults. The differentiation, function, and survival of cellular populations within the skin often rely on the autophagic machinery, and how these unique populations of the skin coordinate to protect are of great interest (66). Moreover, many dermatological disorders are associated with autophagic defects, such as psoriasis (ATG16L1, AP1S3) and vitiligo (ATG7, UVRAG) (66). UV treatment has been successfully used to reduce inflammation associated with skin pathologies, such as psoriasis (25). A recent study reported that increased UV light exposure in the first 3 months of life correlated with a lower incidence of medically diagnosed eczema and decreased levels of IL-2, GM-CSF, and eotaxin, all immune factors associated with allergic inflammation (67). Interestingly, immunosuppression by UV was independent of its ability to convert 7-Dehydrocholesterol (7-DHC) to vitamin D3, though the mechanisms underlying UV’s suppression of eczema remain undefined (67). It has also been reported that adoptive transfer of myeloid cells from UV-irradiated mice into naïve hosts resulted in immunosuppression in a model of CHS, due to altered DNA methylation and reduced antigen presentation capacity in the UV-exposed myeloid cells (68). Despite these immunologically beneficial characteristics, UV exposure represents an environmental risk factor strongly associated with skin cancer, including basal cell carcinoma and malignant melanoma (9). Our work demonstrates that Rubcn deficiency pre-disposes mice to inflammation in response to UV, though it remains to be seen if this heightened inflammation can protect the host against skin carcinogenesis (20).

The execution of apoptotic cell death and their immunologically silent clearance via efferocytosis are processes key to the homeostatic maintenance of an organism, and defects in efferocytosis have also been associated with aberrant and unresolved inflammation (3). The molecular mechanisms by which UV induces an immunosuppressive program are not fully elucidated, yet it is hypothesized that the clearance of apoptotic cargo by phagocytic cells mediates the anti-inflammatory response via Liver X Receptor (LXR) activation, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor (PPAR) signaling, and other cholesterol/lipid sensing pathways (2, 32). While Rubcn-deficient phagocytes are able to engulf dying cells, they cannot fully process and degrade their cellular cargo and fail to initiate an immunotolerant program associated with efferocytosis (4, 6). Previous work (5, 69, 70) has demonstrated, that during LAP, RUBCN binds to canonical autophagy members, VPS34, BECN1, and UVRAG, as well as components of the NOX2 complex, though RUBCN has also been described to interact with RAB7 to inhibit endosomal maturation (34), possibly as a mechanism to inhibit autophagy (5). Other components of non-canonical autophagy, like ATG5, have been implicated in type I interferon signaling by promoting the formation of the LC3-IKKα complex on the LAPosome, highlighting its possible role in antiviral immunity (71). How Rubcn−/− mice respond to infection with viruses, such as herpes simplex virus (HSV), remains an active area of research.

Consistent with our previous work (4), transcriptional analysis reveals that UV pre-treatment of Rubcn−/− skin induces genetic programs consistent with hyperactivation of both innate and adaptive immunity, such as antigen presentation. Interestingly, expression of these pro-inflammatory genes is lower basally in Rubcn−/− skin, compared to Rubcn+/+ skin, suggesting that RUBCN’s ability to negatively regulate canonical autophagy (5, 69) could also serve an immunoregulatory function. During CHS, we demonstrate that the function of Rubcn in dDC2s is critical, where its activity limits antigen presentation and co-stimulatory pathways, and Rubcn-deficiency in dDC2s results in increased inflammation and activation of CD8+ T cells, the primary effector in the CHS model. Rubcn−/− phagocytes retain engulfed cargo in less degradative vesicles due to a failure to fuse with lysosomes (6), and this phenomenon has been associated with increased capacity for MHCI-mediated antigen presentation and activation of CD8+ T cells (72). In addition, DCs deficient for Atg5 or Atg7 display increased surface levels of MHCI, and lipidation of LC3 can promote MHCI internalization and degradation (73). Collectively, this suggests that the exclusion of cargo from lysosomes by Rubcn−/− DCs enhances its ability to present antigen via MHCI, a function perhaps further amplified by the pro-inflammatory cytokine environment (IL-12, IL-1β) associated with Rubcn deficiency.

Other models of skin irritation have demonstrated that dDC2s rapidly migrate from the affected skin to the dLNs, suggesting that they act as sentinels of skin homeostasis (74). Once in the dLNs, it has been reported that dDC2s can also transfer antigen to other DC subsets, such as CD8α+ dLN-resident DCs for efficient T cell activation (75). Depletion of all CD11c+ cells with the CD11c.DTR transgenic mouse results in reduced cross-presentation to CD8+ T cells (76). In our studies, we observe that UV treatment activates a hyper-inflammatory program in skin-resident Rubcn−/− dDC2s, and upon sensitization during the CHS reaction, Rubcn−/− dDC2s demonstrate increased migration to the dLNs and increased antigen presentation capacity.

Further, we used the conditional Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice to demonstrate that ablation of Rubcn in dDCs mimicked the phenotype observed in the constitutive knockout, thus establishing the prominent role of Rubcn in dDCs upon challenge. Interestingly, we observed that generation of Treg cells was preserved in UV+ DNFB-treated Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice, suggesting that cells not affected by Cd11c-Cre-mediated deletion, such as LCs, are involved in Treg generation (77). Studies using LC depletion models, such as the Langerin-Cre+ mouse model, however, have demonstrated diminished inflammation in a CHS model, suggesting a role for LCs in the generation of effector cells as well (48, 78, 79). However, dDCs repopulate the skin more efficiently post-sensitization, compared to LCs, allowing dDCs a prime location during elicitation (75). Our study indicates that while LAP in LCs could be critical for the generation of inhibitory Treg cells, this function plays only a moderate role in limiting the inflammation triggered by UV exposure in the Rubcn−/− mice. Considering our data in the context of LC-specific deletion of LAP, it is possible that the Langerin-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mouse may also display increased inflammation due to aberrant Treg generation. As Treg cells are critical for remodeling the immune microenvironment and inflammation resolution, Rubcn deficiency in the context of other organ system pathogenesis, such as the brain (80), warrants further research.

Studies with mast cell-deficient mice (W/Wv) have demonstrated that mast cells can play a critical role in UV-induced inflammation (81, 82). The release of histamines during mast cell degranulation is an important signal to keratinocytes to produce prostanoids, and in vivo studies with histamine receptor antagonists suggest a role for the histamineprostanoid axis in UV-induced immunosuppression (83). While we observed that the recruitment of mast cells was mediated independently of Rubcn and did not appear to correlate to a reduction in ear swelling, a role for Rubcn and LAP in mast cell activity remains unknown.

Previous studies have demonstrated a single nucleotide deletion in RUBCN, resulting in a frameshift of the C-terminal domain (p.Ala943ValfsX146), was identified as a causative factor in the pathogenesis of a new form of recessive ataxia, Salih ataxia (84, 85). Efforts to identify causative SNPs in RUBCN in other vulnerable populations are ongoing and will help shed new light on how the integrity of autophagic pathways protects against unwanted inflammation, as well as how treatment options intersect with these genetic factors. Analysis of human skin cellular subsets revealed that RUBCN was preferentially expressed in CD141+ dDCs, CD1c+ dDCs, and LCs. Human CD141+ DCs and CD1c+ DCs show similar activation profiles in vivo and can stimulate CD8+ T cells and Th17 response, respectively (86). Our results corroborate these findings and advocate for a dDC2-specific role of Rubcn in shaping the immune environment. Our study demonstrates that this aspect can be leveraged for immunotherapy and vaccine-related therapeutic strategies, as the ability to target LAP in specific cellular subsets can have drastic effects on the immune outcome and can profoundly impact the resolution of inflammation. This can potentially open up a new realm of future therapeutic interventions aimed at resolving inflammation-related disorders.

caption.p: Figure S1: Effect of Rubcn deficiency on inflammatory gene expression in response to UV exposure. (A) RNA from untreated back skin of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice was analyzed via Nanostring nCounter Immunology Codeset. Heatmap data, generated by R studio, depicting average row z-score with significant difference in Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice were exposed to UV. (B) Fold change of significant genes upregulated in UV-treated Rubcn−/− back skin. (C) Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− MEFs were exposed to 20 mJ/cm2 UVB light. After 2 hours, cells were analyzed for phospho-H2A.X (Ser139) via immunofluorescence. Representative images are shown. Percent of positive nuclei and mean fluorescence of immunostaining was calculated using Fiji. (D) Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− MEFs were labeled with SYTOXGreen, exposed to 20 mJ/cm2 UVB light, and live cell imaging was obtained for 24 hours post-UV. Representative images at 0 and 24 hours are shown. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated with two-way ANOVA with MCT (B-C) or using linear regression analysis (D) (*p<0.05).

Figure S2: Resolution of inflammation by Rubcn−/− mice in response to UV exposure in a model of CHS. (A) Ear swelling in vehicle-treated ears from Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice. (B) DNFB-treated ears stained with Masson’s trichrome at 48 hours post-elicitation. (C) Ear swelling of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB or pre-exposed to 3 doses of UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation. Dotted line denotes baseline. (D) Ear swelling of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB (DNFB only) or pre-exposed to UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation (UV+DNFB) over 12 days. % change in ear thickness calculated as UV+DNFB measurement divided by DNFB only measurement. (E) Ear swelling in vehicle-treated ears from Ulk1+/+ and Ulk1−/− mice. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using Student’s t-test (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S3: Inflammatory response of Rubcn−/− mice in response to UV+DNFB treatment. (A) RNA from UV+DNFB treated ear skin was analyzed via Nanostring nCounter Immunology Codeset. Heatmap data, generated by R studio, depicting row z-score are shown. (B-C) Gating strategy for the flow cytometry analysis of innate immune cells in the skin.

Figure S4: Effect of Rubcn deficiency on innate immune cell infiltration in response to UV+DNFB treatment. (A-E) Representative flow cytometry plots and percentage of live MΘs (A), NΘs (B) , mast cells (C), LCs (D), dDC1s (E), and dDC2s (F). Average mean fluorescence of RUBCN in immune cell subsets in the skin as determined by flow cytometry. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated with two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S5: Effect of Rubcn deficiency on T cell infiltration and activation in response to UV+DNFB treatment. (A) Representative flow cytometry plots and percentage of live cells of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells. (B-F). Representative histograms of IFNγ+ CD8+ T cells (B), IFNγ+ CD4+ Th1 cells (C), IL-17+ CD4+ Th17 cells (D), FOXP3+ CD4+ Treg cells (E), and IL-4+ CD4+ Th2 cells (F). DNFB-treated ears were harvested 48 hours post-elicitation and analyzed via flow cytometry for IL-4+ CD4+ Th2 cells. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated with two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S6: Effect of Rubcn deficiency on T cell activation in response to UV+DNFB treatment. (A) Schematic of adoptive transfer of naïve CD45.1+ CD4+ T cells into Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice post-UV exposure, followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation. (B) Ear swelling Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice after CD4+ T cell adoptive transfer at 24 hours post-elicitation. Dotted line denotes baseline. (C-D) Naïve wild type CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells were adoptively transferred into Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice post-UV exposure, followed by DNFB sensitization. After 48 hours, dLNs were analyzed via flow cytometry for presence and activation status of CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells. Representative flow cytometry plots for CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells are shown (D). Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance of ear swelling was calculated using Student’s t-test. Significance for flow cytometry data was calculated using two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S7: Effect of post-sensitization UV exposure on immunosuppression. (A) Schematic of sensitization, UV exposure, and elicitation in Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice. (B) Ear swelling Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice with post-sensitization UV exposure. Dotted line denotes baseline. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using Student’s t-test (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S8: Effect of Rubcn deficiency on activation of innate immune cells post-sensitization. (A-D) Forty-eight hours after sensitization, dLNs were analyzed via flow cytometry for presence and activation status of LCs (A), dDC1s (B), dDC2s (C), and T cells (D). Representative histograms for CD86, MHC-I, and MHC-II for LCs, dDC1s, and dDC2s is shown. (E) PCA plot of gene expression of sorted Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− dDC2s (circles) and Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− LCs (triangles) 48 hours post-sensitization. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S9: Generation and validation of Cd11c-Cre Rubcnflox/flox mice. (A) Targeting scheme for the generation of Rubicon conditional null mice (B) Flow cytometry analysis of RUBCN expression in immune cell subsets in the skin of Rubcnflox/flox and Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice. Representative histograms for RUBCN for each cell subset are shown. Mean fluorescence of RUBCN was calculated for all subsets. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S10: Effect of LAP deficiency in LysM+ and CD11c+ populations in a model of CHS and UV exposure. (A-B) Ear swelling of Rubcnflox/flox and LysM-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB (DNFB only) or pre-exposed to UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation (UV+DNFB). Dotted line denotes baseline. % change in ear thickness calculated as UV+DNFB measurement divided by DNFB only measurement. (C-D) Ear swelling of Atg7flox/flox mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB (DNFB only) or pre-exposed to UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation (UV+DNFB). Dotted line denotes baseline. % change in ear thickness calculated as UV+DNFB measurement divided by DNFB only measurement. (E) Linear regression analysis of % dDC2s versus % CD8+ T cells in ears of UV+DNFB-treated Rubcnflox/flox and Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice post-elicitation. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using Student’s t-test (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

Figure S11: Expression of genes in canonical autophagy and LAP in human dermal dendritic cells and Langerhans cells. (A-C) Re-analysis of gene expression microarray data of isolated from skin biopsies of healthy volunteers and sorted into CD141+ DCs (filled circle), CD1c+ DCs (open circle), CD14+ DCs (filled square), LCs (open square), and Mθs (filled triangle). Microarray gene expression data were used to analyze expression of autophagy- and LAP-dependent genes in each subset. Color scheme represents (A) autophagy- and LAP-dependent genes (green, n=3–6), (B) autophagy only-dependent genes (blue, n=3–6), and (C) LAP only-dependent gene (red; n = 3–6). Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using two-way ANOVA with MCT (**p<0.01).

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: The authors thank the animal husbandry staff at NIEHS, as well as the Clinical Pathology Group in the Cellular & Molecular Pathology Branch at NIEHS. The authors also thank Kevin Gerrish, Rick Fannin, Laura Wharey, and Stella Sieber of the Molecular Genomics Core (NIEHS); Carl Bortner, Maria Sifre, and Kevin S. Katen of the Flow Cytometry Core (NIEHS); Natasha Clayton, Pamela E. Ovwigho, Heather Jensen, and Eli Ney from the Pathology Support Group [NIEHS/ National Toxicology Program (NTP)]; and C.J. Tucker and Erica Scappiani of the Fluorescence Microscopy and Imaging Center (NIEHS). We would also like to thank Donald N. Cook, Michael Fessler, Carol Trempus, Seddon Thomas, Keiko Nakano, and Hideki Nakano (NIEHS) for thoughtful insights and discussions. We also thank Mao Yang, Pat Fitzgerald, Emilio Boada Romero (SJCRH), and Michael Johnson (University of Arizona). This work was supported by NIH Intramural Research Program 1ZIAES10328601 (to J.M.), 1R01AI13920701, Physician-Scientist Strong Start Award, and Duke Dermatology Department Funding (to A.S.M.).

Declaration of Funding Sources: This work was supported by NIH Intramural Research Program 1ZIAES10328601 (to J.M.), 1R01AI13920701, Duke Physician-Scientist Strong Start Award, and Duke Dermatology Department Funding (to A.S.M.).

The authors declare no competing interests.

caption.title: The effect of Rubcn deficiency on initiating the UV-mediated immunotolerant program.
caption.p: Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice were exposed to UV as described in Methods. (A) Rubcn mRNA expression (via RT-PCR, normalized to Gapdh expression) and RUBCN protein expression (via flow cytometry) of basal and UV-exposed wild type animal back skin. (B) RNA from basal and UV-exposed skin was analyzed via Nanostring nCounter Immunology Codeset. Heatmap data, generated by R studio, depicting row z-score are shown of genes with significant difference in expression post-UV exposure. (C-E) UV-treated skin stained for H&E (C), TUNEL (D), or cleaved caspase-3 (E). (F) Scoring of H&E-stained sections on a 5-point severity scale (0 = absent, 1 = minimal, 2 = mild, 3= moderate, 4 = marked). Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated Student’s t-test (A) and two-way ANOVA with MCT (B, F) (*p<0.05, **p<0.01).

caption.title: The requirement of LAP, but not canonical autophagy, in UV-mediated immunosuppression in a model of CHS.
caption.p: (A-B) Ear swelling of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB (DNFB only) or pre-exposed to UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation (UV+DNFB). Dotted line denotes baseline. % change in ear thickness calculated as UV+DNFB measurement divided by DNFB only measurement. (C-D) DNFB-treated ears stained with H&E, TUNEL, or anti-CTGF antibody at 48 hours post-elicitation. Scoring of H&E sections on a 5-point severity scale (0 = absent, 1 = minimal, 2 = mild, 3= moderate, 4 = marked). (E-F) Ear swelling of Ulk1+/+ and Ulk1−/− mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB (DNFB only) or pre-exposed to UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation (UV+DNFB). Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated using Student’s t-test (A-B, E-F) or using ANOVA with MCT (C-D) (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

caption.title: The role of Rubcn in immune cell infiltration in response to UV exposure in a model of CHS.
caption.p: (A-B) DNFB-treated ears were harvested 48 hours post-elicitation and analyzed via flow cytometry for Mθs, Nθs, mast cells in (A) and LCs, dDC1s, and dDC2s in (B). (C) At 48 hours post-elicitation, DNFB-treated skin was stained for CD301b (green) and DAPI (blue). Dotted line denotes articular cartilage. Mean fluorescence of CD301b staining was obtained from images using Fiji. (D) DNFB-treated ears were harvested 48 hours post-elicitation and analyzed via flow cytometry for CD8+ T cells. (E) At 48 hours post-elicitation, DNFB-treated skin was harvested, embedded in paraffin, sectioned, and stained for CD8 (red) and DAPI (blue). Dotted line denotes articular cartilage. Mean fluorescence of CD8 staining was obtained from images using Fiji. (F) DNFB-treated ears were harvested 48 hours post-elicitation and analyzed via flow cytometry for CD4+ T cells. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated with two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

caption.title: T cell-extrinsic role for Rubcn during UV-mediated immunosuppression.
caption.p: (A) Schematic of adoptive transfer of naïve CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells into Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice post-UV exposure, followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation. (B) Ear swelling of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice after CD8+ T cell adoptive transfer at 24 hours post-elicitation. Dotted line denotes baseline. DNFB-treated ears were harvested 24 hours post-elicitation and analyzed via flow cytometry for presence and activation status of CD45.1+ CD8+ T cells. (C) Schematic of adoptive transfer of DNFB (hapten)-specific CD8+ T cells into Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice +/− UV pre-exposure, followed by DNFB elicitation. (D) Ear swelling in both DNFB- and vehicle-treated ears of Rubcn+/+ and Rubcn−/− mice after hapten-specific CD8+ T cell adoptive transfer at 24 hours post-elicitation. Dotted line denotes baseline. (E) Adoptively transferred CD8+ T cells were analyzed by flow cytometry in both DNFB- and vehicle-treated ears. Mean fluorescence of CD44 and IFNγ calculated for Tag-It+ CD8+ T cells. (F) Proliferation peaks were analyzed via dilution of Tag-It Violet™ dye of transferred CD8+ T cells. Representative flow cytometry histogram is shown. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance for ear swelling data was calculated with Student’s t-test. Significance for flow cytometry data was calculated with two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

caption.title: The effect of Rubcn deficiency in the activation and antigen presentation by CD301b+ dDC2s during sensitization.
caption.p: (A-C) Forty-eight hours after sensitization, dLNs were harvested and analyzed via flow cytometry for presence and activation status of LCs, dDC1s, and dDC2s. Mean fluorescence of CD86, MHCI, and MHCII was calculated for all subsets. Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance was calculated with two-way ANOVA (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001). (D-E) Forty-eight hours after sensitization, dDC2s and LCs were sorted from dLNs of UV+DNFB-treated mice. RNA was isolated as described, and differential expression was assayed using Agilent Whole Mouse Genome 4×44 Microarray. Heatmap data, generated by R studio, depicting row z-score are shown.

caption.title: The role of Rubcn in CD11c+ cells during UV-mediated suppression of inflammation.
caption.p: (A-B) Ear swelling of Rubcnflox/flox and Cd11c-Cre+ Rubcnflox/flox mice sensitized and elicited with DNFB (DNFB only) or pre-exposed to UV followed by DNFB sensitization and elicitation (UV+DNFB). Dotted line denotes baseline. % change in ear thickness calculated as UV+DNFB measurement divided by DNFB only measurement. (C) After 48 hours, DNFB-treated ears were analyzed via flow cytometry for presence and activation status of LCs, dDC1s, and dDC2s. Mean fluorescence of CD86 was calculated for all subsets. (D-E) After 48 hours, DNFB-treated ears were analyzed via flow cytometry for presence and activation status of CD8+ T cells (D) and CD4+ T cells (E). Data are expressed as mean ± SEM. Significance for ear swelling data was calculated with Student’s t-test. Significance for flow cytometry data was calculated with two-way ANOVA with MCT (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).

list-item.p: RUBCN is an important mediator of UV-induced immunotolerant state.
RUBCN is required in dDC2s to limit antigen presentation during CHS.
Targeted modulation of non-canonical autophagy could provide therapeutic benefit in skin inflammatory models.
